Issue 	No.1 0 • May 2012
How To Sell Your Product
Without Making A Single Claim
Or Mentioning Any Benefits
Hey Playa,
We got lots to plow through this month. So drop your socks and grab your crocs --if
you can't make more (lots more!) sales from this issue then I'm going to have to ask you to
surrender your marketing license. First up... I am going to show you the art and craft of
selling without selling. Very important in emails. One problem people have when writing
emails is they think they're writing a sales letter. They think they have to load it with
benefits. And they think they have to use all the copywriting tricks and choke holds. But
really, the opposite is true in many cases. What you REALLY should be talking about is ...
the problem your readers want solved. To paraphrase Bill Clinton's cajun guy: "It's the
problem., stupid." That goes for me, too, and I'm as guilty of forgetting this at times as
anyone. Anyway, what I mean is to get inside their "world" and just rap about the painful
problem they're experiencing (or COULD experience if something isn't done to deal with
the situation pronto). Yes, you still make a call to action. But you're not hammering them
with benefits --you're hammering them with the pain they arc feeling or could feel if they
don't find a solution. And since YOU arc the person emailing them each day (you arc
mailing them daily, right?) they will likely look to you as the person to help get rid of said
problem. Here is an example from one of my lists:

Subject Line: Can prostatitis make your penis fall off
Hi,
Prostatitis is truly gruesome.
And yesterday's bit on the subject prompted some questions from
people who think they **might** be starting to have it but are not
sure. 	Since I'm no doctor 	(nor do I 	play one on the Internet) take
what I 	say with a grain of salt.
But one thing I 	DO know about is the pain.
Prostatitis symptoms are extremely painful.
And it can be especially painful on your penis.
How so?
Well, 	take this man I 	knew of with the condition.
He was 	38 years old, 	and his prostatitis symptoms included constant
pain in the tip of his penis. 	He felt excruciating pain every time
he ejaculated or even got an erection. And, 	the pain in his penis,
testicles, 	and anus had gotten so bad he was taking warm baths
several times per day 	(and missing a lot of work), 	and resorted to
sleeping with a pillow between his legs to try and help with the
discomfort.
And that was 	just the beginning.
He had a lot of urinary problems, 	too.
Like a frequent urge to urinate 	(and that also hurt like hell).
Seeing BLOOD in his urine.
And, 	the pain would shift to his lower back and abdomen.
But mostly, 	the penis pain was worst.
He said it felt like it was going to fall off!
Freaky, 	isn't it?
Let's face it, 	that's one part of the body you do NOT want any

See how that works? There isn't a single benefit or claim in that entire email. Yet it
does a better "selling" job than if I'd pummeled them with benefits and bullet points.
Your homework assignment for this month is to write a "pain" email like this. Every
product solves some problem. Your job is to think of all the pains your product solves (or
all the desires it grants) and work them into an email. Use the above as a template (you
may have to play around with it, but the structure is there) and watch what happens.
How Potty-Mouthed Movie Geeks
Write The Best E:mail Subject Lines
Let's talk about subject lines, shall we?
I'm going to hand you one of the BEST subject line tips you'll ever find anywhere.
In fact, if you simply apply the following to your emails, I don't think you'll ever have
trouble thinking up a great subject line for the rest of your life. Tall order? We'll see ...
Check this out:
Sometimes I read imdb.com -- specifically, the forums (yeah, yeah, I know, kinda
dorky haha). Anyway, what's most amusing is some of the spam threads or just when
people are being smart asses. For example, when reading the forums for the next Batman
movie coming out this summer, here are some funny/smart ass/weird thread titles I saw.

Many of them could probably be adapted (or even used outright, in some cases) for
writing curiosity subject lines people will want to open, or else what I call "shock effect"
email subject lines to jolt people into reading because they're so... nutzo ...
I 	promise I'm not 	just saying this to be an ass hole
Some of you are SO racist its 	just sad, 	and this is 20111
Am I 	unattractive?????????
What if Bane rapes Batman? 	(srs)
Christopher nolan will give us herpes.
Bane shoots drugs in his testicles.
This 	'man' 	is a mind-control robot.
What turned you into a racist?
What brand of Malt Liquor would Batman drink?
I 	look like a horse
I 	can't believe I 	didn't notice this before! 	How about You?
guys ••• i 	dont tthink i 	en read it [NOTE: 	this had a TON of views]
Things/people that annoy you on Facebook?
Again, these are very strange subject line ideas. So use common sense, of course
when using/ adapting them. The point is, if you want ideas and inspiration for suq_ject
lines, zip on over to imdb.com and go to the comic book movie forums. Then read the
thread titles. Not all will be usable-- but I've gotten a TON of ideas doing this, and it's
my go-to resource when I'm stuck for ideas.

THE 	E:\-IAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
If you want to know what the true essence of selling with email is (and this probably
applies to most other kinds of selling -- certainly any kind of copywriting type sales) it's
this: Be very SPECIFIC about the probletn... and very VAGUE about the
solution. This dovetails into not doing a lot of hard teaching but still making your emails
informational (and thus, interesting and readable). Almost everyone gets this wrong. And
even when people do get it right, it's usually on complete accident. But do this you must if
you want to make the maximum amount of sales with email on an ongoing basis. In fact,
if you master doing this (being specific about the problem and vague about the solution)
you'll never have to worry about sales ever again. People will find your emails almost
impossible not to keep reading, and will buy from you without hesitation. It takes practice
(and I need it as much as anyone). And it takes a certain mindset shift if you've been
indoctrinated with the whole moving-the-free-line shtick popularized by the goo-roos.
Now, for an example. The email below kicked gluteus bootyus when it originally ran (and
it WILL be re-used). It is a solid example of what I mean by writing emails that are
informational, but not hard teaching. And that are specific about the problem, but very
vague about the solution (they have to get my product for the solution, but they have been
educated, and feel it was worth their time to read). Anyway, here's the example:
Subject line: 	Exercises that fry your brain
Heyal
Remember those old "just say no" to drugs commercials, where
someone would show you an egg and say "this is your brain" then
crack it open into a sizzling frying pan, 	"this is your brain on
drugs."
well, 	guess what?
Exercise can do the SAME thing 	(in a way).
But they do it to your central nervous system.
Here's what I mean:
When you exercise long and hard 	(and to failure) 	you burn out 	("fry")
your central nervous system.
This is badl
Why?

Because when your central nervous system gets burned out 	(fried!)
it basically shuts down a bit and says to your body, 	"No, 	I'm not
going to give you all I 	got. 	I 	can't handle it anymore. 	I'm taking a
break and so are you ••• "
At which point it makes you feel tired.
Drained.
And achey.
Plus, weight loss halts.
Do you see why most exercising is BAD?
Why women will torment themselves for months in a gym only to lose
just a few pounds 	(or even GAIN weight)???
So what's the solution?
Exercise in a way your central nervous system likes.
That's what my ____ book does.
It shows you how to exercise in a secret way 	(not 1 in 1,000
"fitness experts" knows) 	that's not only quick and easy ••• 	but also
fun and that makes your central nervous system HAPPY.
The result:
A toned, 	sexy body.
Short workouts 	(you can do during TV commercials if you want).
And never being tired or achey.
Instead, 	you have a lot MORE energy during the day!
To see how it works, 	go to:

How To Use ''Vintage'' Ads
From 50+ Years Ago
To Write Profitable Emails Today
I'm not the world's biggest advocate of "swipe & steal" copywriting of any kind.
Especially for emails. In fact, I think swiping for emails is not only going to hurt your
sales, but could hurt your reputation, too (especially when done from emails in the same
market). But, that docs NOT mean I don't believe in using tried-and-true ads from
decades ago to help you write your emails today. As long as the appeals are still working
(and many of them still are) then there's no reason why you can't crack open your swipe
file of old ads and see about recycling their themes and ideas for your emails. Take the
long-running mega-profitable ad "Why Men Crack" for example. It is reproduced on the
last page of this newsletter (go ahead and read it real quick, I'll be here when you get back
-- hurry, get crackin'). Did you read it? Okay, then here is how I used its headline and
opening and ideas to write a profitable email recently (looking back tl1is is pretty sloppily
written, but it made sales, which goes to show the power of doing this):
Subject Line: Why women crack
You know them, 	right?
Strong women, 	healthy, 	thin, 	with their
whole lives ahead of them. 	But thanks to society and stress, 	they
drive themselves to the limit and harm their health and gain weight
with the standard westernized unhealthy foods.
What happens is, 	they "crack."
And eventually ••• 	crash.
That's when the weight gain starts.
It doesn't happen overnight.
And those closest to them 	(husbands, 	boyfriends, 	etc) 	don't even

Again, that was a sloppy email (I wrote it very fast). But it still worked. And the good
news is, there are literally thousands of old, vintage ads you can do this with. Just look up
old ads selling to your market (or a similar market) and recycle the ideas and themes for
your emails (don't blatantly steal, especially since some of them may still be copyrighted--
just let them inspire and give you ideas like I did for the above email). A couple great
resources for these ads is Michael Senofrs hardtofindads.com and Lawrence Bernstein's
infomarketingblog.com. Also, one of my "secret weapons" for this is an old book of ads
called "The World's 100 Greatest Advertisements" by Julian Watkins. It was written back
in 1959 and you can sometimes find it on eBay or Amazon.com.

I've been finding this to work like crazy (doing TWO of these
concurrently for our weight loss site from Nov 23-27 this month -- one
selling to ___ subscribers offering a discount and the other to
buyers launching the new 	book):
Day 1:
Day 2:
Day 3:
Day 4:
Email #1 	(morning) 	announcement of sale
Email #2 	(evening) Reminder of the sale
Email #1 	(morning) 	informational email 	(content/entertainment)
that ties strongly into the sale
Email #2 	(night) 	reminder of sale
Email #1 	(morning) 	informational email 	(content/entertainment)
that ties strongly into the sale
Email #2 	(night) 	reminder of sale, 	with emphasis on the looming
deadline
Email #1 	(5 am) 	informational email 	(content/entertainment) 	that
ties strongly into the sale with emphasis on looming deadline at
midnight
Email #2 	(12 noon) 	reminder of sale coming down at midnight
Email #3 	(3 pm) 	short email that's pure entertaining 	(all above
the fold, 	make a 	joke or something) 	that then plugs the sale and
emphasizes the deadline at midnight
Email #4 	(5 pm) 	tell them they only have 7 more hours, 	if you can
throw in some high credibility testimonials 	(here's what so and
so says ••• ) that's ideal, 	strong call to action and reminder of
deadline

main list while mailing the sublist with 7 emails).
There you have it. I don't know how to make it any easier and simple to follow than
that ...
Why Nothing Bad
Ever Happens
To An E~nail Writer...
I make no mystery of the fact my main influence with email is the great Matt Furey.
Some people don't like his most recent stuff, but I can tell you his older stuff is primo
awesomeness. Anyway, one of the things he used to say was, "nothing bad happens to an
email writer." By that he meant, everything that happens to you is an email. If you have a
flat tire, write about it. Got pulled over for speeding? Write an email about it and tie it
into selling your product. Having a spat of erectile dysfunction? Hey, sac up and write
about it (haha I remember him using that analogy in his original email course). Anyway, I
have found this to be true, as well. People can relate and identify with problems, especially
common problems they have suffered from or think they could suffer from. So now I want
to give you the ULTIMATE example of turning a bad experience into an email designed
to sell. My friendjim Yaghi wrote this when his grandpa died. What is more tragic than a
family member dying? Yet he turned it into a great email to sell his product (tastefully) ..

•ey, 	times like this ••• it's hard to think business.
my grandfather passed away this week:(.
We were close--and it was very sudden. 	he was TOUGH AS NAILS though-- a

down later that night without so much as a whince of pain.
as i 	grieved my grandfather, 	and dealt with his funeral, 	the
IBA launch had begun and nothing i 	could do would stop it.
i 	BURIED him--
meanwhile, 	the phone in my pocket vibrated with sales notifications.
i 	didn't even have the heart or energy to sit at the computer for days.
but today, 	as i 	looked over the sales figures and website stats-- IT
SANK IN--
how blessed i 	aml
most people would not have been able to run a launch, 	let alone make a
sale.
but i 	did it. 	though i 	didn't sit at the computer.
everything was on autopilot. 	promo emails were all pre-written weeks in
advance--sales mechanisms had been tested and implemented--leads
generated themselves. my staff had already been instructed to hit
BUTTONS and publish blog posts, 	share them, 	and send out promo emails.
me?
i 	was UN-INVOLVED.
we welcomed guests at the family home for three days straight--from
morning till lam. 	it was an exhausting but beautiful ceremony--a
celebration of his life. 	this was a man who struggled to his dying
breath just to make a good life for his children and theirs.
i 	want to share this blessing with YOU.
there are going to be days ahead, 	when life ••• sickness ••• or death ••• get
in the way. 	days when you can't get out of bed--or when your computer

How's that for a "turning lemons into lemonade" email? Notice the emotion, the
flow and the structure. It's just a great example of how to turn something bad into an
email that is designed to make you more of the green stuff. Now, that doesn't mean you
have to write about death, necessarily. Again, ANYTHING bad that happens is an email
--big or small. It doesn't even have to be something "bad." It can just be an aggravation
or inconvenience or annoyance. The trick is logically and naturally (and tastefully)
-13-

segueing into the sales pitch, which you can get good at simply by doing it as often as

Subject Line: 	Prostate pills that cause skin rashes
Hello •••
I'm a big fan of zinc.
I 	think every man should get tested to make sure you're not low on
zinc, 	especially if you are suffering from any prostate problems.
But, 	zinc isn't without drawbacks.
For example:
I've been taking zinc for over 2 years now.
However, 	I might be taking too much.
You see, 	too much zinc can interfere with your body's ability to
absorb copper 	(which you also need). 	And when this happens you can
have all kinds of other health problems -- including 	(but not
limited to) 	skin problems.
Well, 	guess what?
For the past few months I've had skin problems.
And, 	that's when I 	started doubling my zinc intake.
Coincidence?
Maybe.
But in my case, 	I 	have itchy, 	flakey skin around my eyes, 	where
just blinking causes these flakes to fall off my face 	(sometimes
landing on an eye lash-- it's driving me crazy).
Anyway, 	normally I'd go to the dermatologist.
And I 	will if it doesn't go away.
But first, 	I'm not taking any zinc this week.
Hopefully that will clear it up.
-14-
